steps.title: Identify what the conclusion requires
steps.content: The team concluded that exposure amount influences language-distinction ability. A finding that supports this must show a direct link: dogs with more exposure (older dogs) display a stronger ability to distinguish their familiar language from an unfamiliar one.

steps.title: Eliminate each wrong choice and confirm the answer
steps.content:
Test all four choices against the exposure-ability requirement:
  • Choice A — 'Although the dogs' general hearing sensitivity declined with age, dogs of all ages showed more brain activity in response to hearing the language they were accustomed to than in response to hearing the other language.': shows all dogs discriminate equally regardless of age, so no link between more exposure and stronger ability. ✗
  • Choice B — 'The difference between the pattern of brain activity a dog showed when hearing the language it was accustomed to and the pattern of brain activity it showed when hearing the language it was not accustomed to was greatest among older dogs.': directly links more exposure (older dogs) to stronger discrimination (largest brain-activity difference), supporting the conclusion. ✓
  • Choice C — 'The similarity between the pattern of brain activity a dog showed in response to hearing the scrambled recording and the pattern of brain activity it showed in response to hearing the language it was not accustomed to was greatest among older dogs.': compares scrambled vs. unfamiliar language responses, not familiar vs. unfamiliar, so it does not address the conclusion. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Dogs showed a different pattern of brain activity when hearing the language they were accustomed to than when hearing the scrambled recording, and the difference in brain activity increased with the age of the dog scanned.': compares familiar language to scrambled recordings, not to an unfamiliar language, so it does not support the conclusion about language distinction. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
